title: How to handle the complexity of forgiving yourself
author: Kalesh
excerpt: You've done something you regret. Maybe it was last week. Maybe it was twenty years ago. The memory still has teeth. It still wakes you up at 3 a.m. with that...
metaDescription: You've done something you regret. Maybe it was last week. Maybe it was twenty years ago. The memory still has teeth. It still wakes you up at 3 a.m. with...
htmlContent:
You've done something you regret. Maybe it was last week. Maybe it was twenty years ago. The memory still has teeth. It still wakes you up at 3 a.m. with that familiar twist in your gut. And you've been told, over and over, that you need to forgive yourself. Just let it go. Move on. Forgive and forget.

But here's the thing. Forgiving yourself when you've caused real harm, when the shame is thick enough to choke on, when your nervous system is locked in a permanent state of self-loathing... that's not a simple decision. It's not a switch you flip. It's a complex, messy, non-linear process that your brain and body might actively resist.

I've been there. I've sat in therapy offices and support groups and my own dark living room at 2 a.m., trying to force myself to forgive. Trying to reason my way out of the shame. And it never worked. Because self-forgiveness isn't a cognitive exercise. It's a full-body, nervous system renegotiation. And your body might not be ready.

So let's talk about what actually happens when you try to forgive yourself. Let's talk about why it's so hard. And let's talk about how to approach it in a way that doesn't just add another layer of failure on top of the original wound.

Why your nervous system won't let you forgive yourself

Here's what I wish someone had told me years ago. Your inability to forgive yourself isn't a moral failing. It's not a lack of spiritual maturity. It's a survival strategy.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When you've done something that violates your own values, your nervous system interprets that as a threat. Not an external threat, but an internal one. A threat to your identity, your sense of being a good person, your place in the tribe.

So your body goes into protection mode. It locks onto the shame. It plays the memory on a loop. It keeps you in a state of hypervigilant self-criticism. And here's the brutal irony: your nervous system is doing this to protect you. It's saying, "If I keep beating myself up, I won't do that again. I'll stay safe. I'll stay accepted."

Self-flagellation becomes a form of control. A way to preemptively punish yourself before anyone else can. A way to prove you're not a monster, because monsters don't feel this much remorse, right?

But it doesn't work. It just keeps you stuck in the shame loop. And the harder you try to force forgiveness, the more your nervous system resists. Because forgiveness, in your body's language, feels like letting your guard down. It feels like saying what you did was okay. And if what you did was truly harmful, your body will fight that with everything it has.

So the first step isn't to forgive yourself. The first step is to understand why your body is holding on so tightly. To recognize that the shame is trying to protect you, even if it's destroying you in the process.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on the inner critic and shame spirals can help you see that your self-attack isn't you. It's a part of you that got stuck in survival mode.

The difference between guilt and shame (and why it matters)

I need you to understand something crucial. Guilt and shame are not the same thing. And confusing them is one of the main reasons self-forgiveness feels impossible.

Guilt says, "I did something bad." Shame says, "I am bad."

Guilt is about behavior. It's a signal that you violated your own values. And guilt can be useful. It can motivate repair. It can lead to change. Guilt says, "I hurt someone, and I need to make it right." That's a clean, actionable message.

Shame is about identity. It's a global condemnation of your entire being. Shame says, "There is something wrong with me. I am defective. I am unworthy of love." And shame doesn't lead to repair. It leads to hiding, numbing, and more self-destruction.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades studying how trauma gets trapped in the body. And one of his key insights is that shame is often the most toxic residue of trauma. Not just the trauma of what was done to you, but the trauma of what you did to others or yourself.

So when you try to forgive yourself while you're drowning in shame, you're asking your brain to do something it can't do. You're asking it to say, "I am good," while every cell in your body is screaming, "I am bad." That's not forgiveness. That's gaslighting yourself.

Real self-forgiveness starts with separating guilt from shame. It starts with looking at what you did, without collapsing into the belief that what you did defines who you are. And that separation takes practice. It takes a nervous system that can tolerate looking at your own mistakes without going into fight-or-flight.

Think about it. When you make a mistake, what happens in your body? Do you feel that tightness in your chest? That heat in your face? That urge to disappear or lash out? That's shame. That's the nervous system flooding you with the message that you are in danger of being rejected, ostracized, cast out.

And until you can regulate that response, you can't access the guilt. You can't access the clear-eyed assessment of what happened and what needs to be repaired. You're just a shame-soaked puddle of self-hatred.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Nedra Glover Tawwab's work on boundaries can help you understand that self-forgiveness often requires setting boundaries with your own inner critic. You get to decide how much airtime that voice gets.

Repair before release

Here's another thing nobody tells you. Sometimes you can't forgive yourself because you haven't actually done the work of repair. You want to skip to the forgiveness part because the guilt is unbearable. But forgiveness isn't a shortcut out of discomfort. It's a destination you arrive at after you've made amends.

I'm not talking about groveling or self-flagellation. I'm talking about concrete, meaningful repair. If you hurt someone, can you apologize without making it about your own guilt? Can you listen to how your actions affected them, without defending yourself or collapsing into shame? Can you change the behavior that caused the harm?

If the person you hurt is no longer in your life, or if contacting them would cause more harm, the repair might look different. It might mean doing the internal work to understand why you did what you did. It might mean making different choices going forward. It might mean living in a way that honors the person you want to become, not the person you were.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist and author who integrates Internal Family Systems (IFS) with trauma treatment, talks about the concept of "earned forgiveness." You can't just decide to forgive yourself. You have to earn it through action. Through showing up differently. Through proving to yourself that you are capable of change.

And that takes time. It takes repetition. It takes failing again and then getting back up. It takes building a track record of new choices that contradict the old story about who you are.

Stay with me here. This is the hard part. You might have to sit in the discomfort of not forgiving yourself for a while. You might have to tolerate the ambiguity of being someone who did something harmful and is also trying to become someone different. That's not a comfortable place to be. But it's an honest one.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work on the stages of recovery can help you understand that reconnection with yourself, including self-forgiveness, comes after safety and remembrance and mourning. You can't rush it.

Working with the parts that won't forgive

I've been doing this work for years. And I still have parts of me that refuse to forgive. Parts that hold onto the shame like a shield. Parts that believe if I forgive myself, I'll lose my motivation to be better. Parts that think I don't deserve forgiveness.

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems (IFS), would call these "protector parts." They're not bad. They're not wrong. They're trying to keep you safe in the only way they know how. And if you try to override them, they'll just get louder.

So instead of trying to force forgiveness, try getting curious about the resistance. Ask yourself: What part of me won't forgive? What is it afraid would happen if I did? What would I lose? What would I have to face?

For me, the part that won't forgive is terrified that if I let go of the shame, I'll become reckless again. It believes that self-hatred is the only thing keeping me in line. And I can't just argue with that part. I have to show it, over and over, that I can make good choices from a place of self-compassion, not self-flagellation.

That's slow work. It's not dramatic. It's not the kind of breakthrough you see in movies. It's just day after day of noticing when the shame voice gets loud, and saying, "I see you. I hear you. You're trying to protect me. But I'm going to try something different now."

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises specifically designed to help you work with the inner critic and shame, which are the main obstacles to self-forgiveness.

What self-forgiveness actually looks like in practice

I want to be honest with you. Self-forgiveness, for me, doesn't feel like a warm wave of love and acceptance. It doesn't feel like a weight lifting off my shoulders. It feels more like... a quiet letting go. A decision to stop adding to the pile of self-punishment. A recognition that the debt I've been trying to pay has no creditor.

It's not a feeling. It's a practice. It's showing up every day and saying, "I did that. It was harmful. I regret it. And I am not that action. I am the person who is trying to learn from it."

It's making amends where possible, and then releasing the outcome. It's accepting that some people may never forgive you, and that's their right. It's accepting that you may never fully forgive yourself, and that's okay too.

Because here's the thing about complex trauma survivors. We often hold ourselves to impossible standards. We expect to be perfect. We expect to never hurt anyone. We expect to have clean, neat, linear recovery. And when we fail at that, we add it to the pile of evidence that we're broken.

But self-forgiveness isn't about being perfect. It's about being human. It's about accepting that you will hurt people, sometimes badly, and that you can still be worthy of love and connection. It's about accepting that your mistakes don't have to be the final word on who you are.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did something relax? Did something tighten? Did a voice say, "But you don't understand what I did"? That's okay. That's part of the process.

Sit with that. Don't try to fix it. Don't try to argue with it. Just notice it. That's the first step. That's the beginning of self-forgiveness.

You don't have to get there today. You don't have to get there tomorrow. You just have to keep showing up, keep doing the work, and keep being curious about what's in the way. The forgiveness will come when it's ready. And so will you.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
